title: RDI pipeline CDC and backpressure behavior
summary: Index of focused causal-flow sections for reasoning about RDI pipeline input, backlog, target writes, backpressure, checkpoint replay, and processing errors.

load_policy.default: Load this index first. Load a focused section only when its routing terms, metrics, or selector-tree outcome match the user question.
load_policy.small_context: Use the selector tree, metric index, or causal_chains summaries before loading a full section.
load_policy.full_context: Load multiple sections only when the user asks a cross-cutting question, such as how target slowdown leads to backpressure and then catch-up.

when: The user reports multiple symptoms, such as lag, target write latency, and backpressure.
The user asks for end-to-end behavior rather than one causal neighborhood.
The root cause is unclear from the user's first question.
cross_cutting_routes.summary: Start from the index and metric index, then inspect target slowdown and backpressure together because downstream target pressure can create backlog and activate backpressure.

question_patterns: How does CDC produce pipeline input?

question_patterns: Is an out-of-memory message during snapshot always an error?

question_patterns: What happens if the target Redis database is slow?

sections.title: Checkpointing and at-least-once replay
question_patterns: How does RDI avoid data loss after processor failure?

sections.title: Rejected records and processing errors
question_patterns: How should processing errors be separated from lag?

sections.title: Metric-to-causal-node index
question_patterns: Which causal-flow section should I use for this alert?
Map a Grafana panel metric to likely RDI causes.

guidance_summary: Backpressure is protective throttling, not data loss by default.

Reason downstream-to-upstream when target writes are slow: target latency constrains throughput, throughput affects backlog, backlog affects memory pressure, and memory pressure can activate backpressure.
Distinguish source lag, stream event latency, and target data freshness.
Separate rejected records and erroneous events from normal backpressure behavior.
Mention at-least-once delivery and idempotent writes when discussing duplicates after restart or reprocessing.
